source_paper_title: The Competition Complexity of Dynamic Pricing
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Brustle_2024, title={The Competition Complexity of Dynamic Pricing}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0230}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0230}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Brustle, Johannes and Correa, José and Duetting, Paul and Verdugo, Victor}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1986–2008} }
context:
Page 9, Section 2.5 (end of Theorem 4 discussion)

Our lower bound for the Competition Complexity in Theorem 4 uses \(m − 1\) MHR distributions and one regular distribution. It remains open whether the Competition Complexity is sublinear for the case of \(m\) independent and MHR stages.
title: Determine sublinear competition complexity for independent monotone hazard rate stages
background_latex:
Consider a dynamic auction with 
1) a single seller, 
2) a fixed set of buyers present in every stage, and 
3) one item sold in each of \(m\) consecutive stages.

In each stage \(k\in\{1,\dots,m\}\), each buyer \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) draws a private value \(v_i^k\) for that stage's item. Values are independent across buyers. Stages are independent in the sense that, for each buyer, the random variables \(v_i^1,\dots,v_i^m\) are independent across \(k\). The (common, i.i.d. across buyers) marginal distribution at stage \(k\) is a continuous distribution \(D_k\) on \(\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) with cumulative distribution function \(F_k\) and density \(f_k\).

A continuous distribution \(D\) with cdf \(F\) and density \(f\) is monotone hazard rate (MHR) if its hazard rate
\[
 h(x)=\frac{f(x)}{1-F(x)}
\]
is nondecreasing in \(x\) on its support.

Let \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) denote the optimal expected revenue achievable by a revenue-maximizing incentive-compatible dynamic mechanism with \(n\) buyers over \(m\) stages when the full stochastic environment is \(X\) (here, independent stages with marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\)). Let \(\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\) denote the expected revenue of running a second-price auction (VCG for a single item) separately in each stage with \(n+c\) buyers, where the extra \(c\) buyers are also i.i.d. from the same stage distributions and participate in every stage.

The competition complexity (with respect to stagewise VCG) is the minimum \(c\ge 0\) such that
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
(Equivalently, the smallest number of additional buyers needed so that simple stagewise second-price auctions match the optimal dynamic auction's expected revenue.)
problem_definition_latex:
In the setting above with independent stages and continuous MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), determine the asymptotic growth (in \(n\)) of the competition complexity \(c\) needed for stagewise VCG to achieve
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
In particular, decide whether there exists a function \(c(n)=o(n)\) (possibly depending on \(m\) but not on the specific MHR distributions) such that for every choice of continuous MHR stage distributions \(D_1,\dots,D_m\) with independent stages,
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c(n),m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
latex:
Consider a dynamic auction with 
1) a single seller, 
2) a fixed set of buyers present in every stage, and 
3) one item sold in each of \(m\) consecutive stages.

In each stage \(k\in\{1,\dots,m\}\), each buyer \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) draws a private value \(v_i^k\) for that stage's item. Values are independent across buyers. Stages are independent in the sense that, for each buyer, the random variables \(v_i^1,\dots,v_i^m\) are independent across \(k\). The (common, i.i.d. across buyers) marginal distribution at stage \(k\) is a continuous distribution \(D_k\) on \(\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) with cumulative distribution function \(F_k\) and density \(f_k\).

A continuous distribution \(D\) with cdf \(F\) and density \(f\) is monotone hazard rate (MHR) if its hazard rate
\[
 h(x)=\frac{f(x)}{1-F(x)}
\]
is nondecreasing in \(x\) on its support.

Let \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) denote the optimal expected revenue achievable by a revenue-maximizing incentive-compatible dynamic mechanism with \(n\) buyers over \(m\) stages when the full stochastic environment is \(X\) (here, independent stages with marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\)). Let \(\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\) denote the expected revenue of running a second-price auction (VCG for a single item) separately in each stage with \(n+c\) buyers, where the extra \(c\) buyers are also i.i.d. from the same stage distributions and participate in every stage.

The competition complexity (with respect to stagewise VCG) is the minimum \(c\ge 0\) such that
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
(Equivalently, the smallest number of additional buyers needed so that simple stagewise second-price auctions match the optimal dynamic auction's expected revenue.)

In the setting above with independent stages and continuous MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), determine the asymptotic growth (in \(n\)) of the competition complexity \(c\) needed for stagewise VCG to achieve
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c,m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
In particular, decide whether there exists a function \(c(n)=o(n)\) (possibly depending on \(m\) but not on the specific MHR distributions) such that for every choice of continuous MHR stage distributions \(D_1,\dots,D_m\) with independent stages,
\[
\mathrm{Rev}[\mathrm{VCG},X,n+c(n),m]\;\ge\;\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m].
\]
open_problem_quote: Our lower bound for the Competition Complexity in Theorem 4 uses \(m − 1\) MHR distributions and one regular distribution. It remains open whether the Competition Complexity is sublinear for the case of \(m\) independent and MHR stages.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search over parametric families of continuous MHR distributions (e.g., piecewise-exponential hazards) and simulate VCG and candidate upper bounds on \(\mathrm{OPT}\) to look for instances that force \(c\) to be linear. However, exactly computing \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) for dynamic mechanisms is generally hard, so the search would rely on relaxations/upper bounds, limiting decisiveness.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary loop could represent stage distributions via hazard-rate parametrizations and attempt to maximize an estimated gap between \(\mathrm{OPT}\) (approximated via LP discretizations or known upper bounds) and stagewise VCG revenue with \(n+c\) buyers, providing a computable fitness signal. The main limitation is that faithfully evaluating \(\mathrm{OPT}\) is computationally expensive and approximation-dependent, so the signal may be noisy or only indicative.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem asks whether, for independent stages with continuous MHR marginals \(D_1,\dots,D_m\), the number of extra buyers \(c\) needed so that stagewise VCG matches the optimal dynamic revenue can be made sublinear in \(n\), uniformly over all such MHR distributions. The source paper (Liu--Psomas) establishes linear upper and lower bounds in more general settings: with correlated stages and MHR marginals, competition complexity is between \((e-1)n\) and \(3n\); with independent stages and \(m-1\) MHR plus one merely regular stage, the same linear bounds hold. Their proofs combine (i) upper bounds on \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) via social welfare and via a dynamic extension of the Cai--Devanur--Weinberg duality framework, and (ii) new inequalities for MHR order statistics, notably \(\mathbb{E}[X_{2:4n}]\ge \mathbb{E}[X_{1:n}]\), which yields the \(3n\) upper bound.

However, the fully independent, all-stages-MHR case remains unresolved: the paper’s linear lower bound construction for exact competition complexity uses either ex-ante IR features or introduces one regular (non-MHR) stage, and thus does not rule out \(c(n)=o(n)\) when every stage is MHR and stages are independent. Progress likely requires either (a) a sharper, independence-specific upper bound on \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) that is strictly below the welfare benchmark by an amount that stagewise VCG can cover with fewer than \(\Theta(n)\) extra buyers, or (b) a new lower-bound construction showing that even under independence and MHR, \(\mathrm{OPT}\) can exceed stagewise VCG by a linear-in-\(n\) gap that cannot be closed with sublinear additional competition. At present, based on the provided materials, the problem should be regarded as open.

sources.title: On the Competition Complexity of Dynamic Mechanism Design
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and studies competition complexity for dynamic auctions. For continuous MHR stage marginals, proves linear upper bound (at most \(3n\)) and linear lower bound (at least \((e-1)n\)) for correlated stages; for independent stages with \(m-1\) MHR and one regular stage, same bounds. Leaves open whether competition complexity can be sublinear when all \(m\) stages are independent and MHR.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open question: whether there exists \(c(n)=o(n)\) such that stagewise VCG with \(n+c(n)\) buyers matches \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) for every choice of independent continuous MHR stage distributions.

sources.title: Auctions Versus Negotiations
sources.theorem_or_result: Bulow--Klemperer theorem: for a single-item auction with \(n\) i.i.d. buyers from a regular distribution, Vickrey with \(n+1\) buyers yields revenue at least the optimal (Myerson) auction with \(n\) buyers.
sources.connection: Provides the static benchmark and key ingredient used in Liu--Psomas to relate VCG revenue (second order statistic) to optimal revenue; motivates seeking small extra competition in dynamic settings.

sources.title: Optimal Auction Design
sources.theorem_or_result: Characterizes revenue-optimal single-item auctions via virtual values; establishes the Myerson auction and the concept of regularity.
sources.connection: Underpins the comparison between VCG revenue and optimal revenue stage-by-stage (via virtual values) and is used in Liu--Psomas duality/virtual welfare benchmarks.

sources.title: On the Complexity of Dynamic Mechanism Design
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows NP-hardness of computing optimal deterministic dynamic mechanisms even for two stages and one buyer; exhibits strong separations between adaptive and non-adaptive dynamic mechanisms and examples where dynamic mechanisms greatly outperform stagewise optimal static auctions.
sources.connection: Provides motivating examples (used by Liu--Psomas) showing that without tail assumptions, stagewise auctions can be far from dynamic OPT, suggesting why competition complexity may need to be large in general.

sources.title: Sequential Mechanisms with Ex-Post Participation Guarantees
sources.theorem_or_result: Characterizes optimal ex-post IR, periodic IC mechanisms for independent-stage sequential auctions; shows existence of optimal mechanisms with zero stage utility except possibly the last, and provides approximation mechanisms for single-buyer settings.
sources.connection: Supplies structural understanding of dynamic OPT under ex-post IR and independent stages, which is central to bounding OPT and comparing to stagewise VCG in the MHR setting.

sources.title: A Duality Based Unified Approach to Bayesian Mechanism Design
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a strong duality framework for Bayesian mechanism design that yields upper bounds on optimal revenue via virtual welfare benchmarks; unifies several approximation results.
sources.connection: Liu--Psomas extend this duality framework to dynamic settings to upper bound \(\mathrm{OPT}[X,n,m]\) for independent stages, a key step in analyzing competition complexity.
